question_text: What are the different geological fracture zones ?

['Chile']: The Chile Fracture Zone (CFZ) is a major  strike slip fault  and  fracture zone  in the  Chile Rise . The Chile Fracture Zone runs in an eastwest direction almost parallel to nearby  Juan Fernandez Ridge  and makes up a large part of the  Antarctic -- Nazca Plate  boundary.
['Mocha']: Mocha Fracture Zone (MFZ) is a  fracture zone  on the  Nazca Plate  off the coast of  Mocha Island ,  Chile . Mocha Fracture Zone trends from the  Valdivia Fracture Zone  in the south to the  Peru-Chile Trench  axis 100 km west of Mocha Island.
['Valdivia']: The Valdivia Fracture Zone (VFZ) is a  transform fault  zone off the coast of  southern Chile  which runs between the continental slope near  Valdivia  in Chile and the  Panov Seamount  crossing the  East Pacific Rise .
['Easter']: The Easter Fracture Zone is an oceanic  fracture zone  associated with the  transform fault  extending from the  Tuamotu archipelago  in the west to the  Peru-Chile Trench  to the east. The Easter Fracture Zone extends roughly 5900 kilometers from 20degS,131degW to 26degS,78degW. The landscape consists of several ridges and isolated  volcano s with maximal peak elevation above the seafloor of 3000m. Because the local seafloor has depths around 4000m to the north of the fracture zone and 3400m to the south of the fracture zone, most of its volcanic peaks form  seamount s. They do rise above sea level at the  Pitcairn Islands  and  Easter Island .
['Vema ']: This narrow ridge must have risen as a single vertical slab 6.6 million years ago. Up to three million years ago the ridge was at or even above  sealevel . Later it  subsided  to its present depth of 450 meters. Lema Fracture Zone. While the transform valley of the Vema Fracture Zone has a V-shaped profile, the next fracture zone to the south has a steep vertical wall on its southern side rising from a horizontal valley bottom. As an L would fit in well here, this fracture zone is nicknamed Lema Fracture Zone. The north-south distance between the Vema and Lema fracture zones is 70 km.

Ridge jump. Up to 2.2 million years ago the Vema transform fault was 40 km shorter than today, and the Lema fracture zone had a leftward offset of 40 km. Then the old spreading valley between Lema and Vema died, and spreading started from an axis 40 km to the east. The Vema transform was thus extended by 40 km, while the Lema fracture zone was reduced to zero offset. Spreading from the new axis has meanwhile increased the distance between the old and the new axis to 80 km.
['Kane ']: The Kane Fracture Zone is a medium-offset  transform fault zone  located on the  Mid-Atlantic Ridge  (MAR). It lies just to the north of the Kane Megamullion, an  oceanic core complex  that forms the  footwall  of a long-lived low-angle detachment fault. The Mid-Atlantic Ridge near the Kane Fracture zone (known as the MARK area) has been the focus of detailed scientific investigations, notably Expeditions 106 and 109 of the  Ocean Drilling Program , as well as numerous other geological and geophysical studies.
['Charlie-Gibbs Name from GEBCO gazettee:
The fault was named Charlie Fracture Zone after the  USCG  Ocean Weather Station Charlie at , athwart the fault. In July 1968 conducted a more extended survey. It was proposed that the fracture zone be renamed Gibbs Fracture Zone, as fracture zones are generally named for research vessels. The proposal was accepted only in part, and currently the official name is Charlie-Gibbs Fracture Zone. Note that the double name refers to the two parallel fracture zones together. The individual fracture zones have to be referred to as Charlie-Gibbs North and South. Seamounts. The transform area contains two named seamounts:
Protected area. The Charlie-Gibbs Marine Protected Area is a conservation area in the Charlie-Gibbs fracture zone in North Atlantic international waters.

Charlie-Gibbs Fracture Zone is a system of two parallel  fracture zone s. It is the most prominent interruption of the  Mid-Atlantic Ridge  between the  Azores  and  Iceland . It can be traced over more than 2000 kilometers, all the way from north-east of  Newfoundland  to south-west of  Ireland . It took 90 million years for the fault to grow to this length. Structure. The  transform fault  of the southern fracture zone displaces the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, coming from the  Azores Triple Junction , to the west over a distance of 120 km. At  longitude  31.75W a south to north  seismically active   rift valley  with a length of 40 km connects the western end of the southern transform to the eastern end of the northern transform.

wikipedia_gemini-2.5-flash_self_containe:
Here are the different geological fracture zones and related entities:

**Fracture (geology)**
A fracture in geology refers to any separation within a geologic formation, such as a joint or a fault, that divides the rock into two or more distinct pieces. These separations can sometimes manifest as deep fissures or crevices in the rock. Fractures are typically formed when stress applied to the rock exceeds its inherent strength, causing it to lose cohesion along its weakest plane. Geologically, fractures are significant because they can enhance permeability, allowing for the movement of fluids like water or hydrocarbons. Highly fractured rocks can therefore serve as effective aquifers or hydrocarbon reservoirs, possessing both substantial permeability and fracture porosity.

**Fault (geology)**
A fault is a specific type of planar fracture or discontinuity found within a volume of rock, characterized by significant displacement across the fracture surface due to rock-mass movements. Large faults within the Earth's crust are primarily a result of plate tectonic forces, with the most substantial ones forming the boundaries between tectonic plates, such as megathrust faults in subduction zones or transform faults. Rapid movement along active faults is the primary cause of most earthquakes, though faults can also displace slowly through a process known as aseismic creep.

**Fault Plane**
The fault plane is the specific planar surface that represents the fracture surface of a fault. It is the actual surface along which the two blocks of rock have moved relative to each other. Understanding the orientation and characteristics of a fault plane is crucial for geologists to analyze the mechanics of faulting and the associated tectonic stresses.

**Fault Trace / Fault Line**
A fault trace, also known as a fault line, is the visible expression of a fault on the Earth's surface. It is the line where the fault plane intersects the ground surface, making it possible for geologists to observe or map the fault. Fault traces are commonly plotted on geological maps to represent the location and orientation of faults.

**Fault Zone**
A fault zone is a geological area characterized by a cluster of parallel faults. Alternatively, the term can also refer to the zone of crushed rock that develops along a single fault due to prolonged motion. When closely spaced faults experience extended movement, the distinction between individual faults can blur, as the rock situated between them is progressively converted into fault-bound lenses of rock and subsequently crushed.

**Clarion–Clipperton fracture zone (CCZ)**
The Clarion–Clipperton fracture zone (CCZ) is a vast environmental management area located in the eastern and central Pacific Ocean, situated between Hawaii and Mexico. It is recognized as one of the world's largest known fields of polymetallic nodules, which are rich in valuable minerals such as manganese, nickel, copper, and cobalt, making it a significant area for potential deep-sea mining. The CCZ is administered by the International Seabed Authority (ISA) under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS). Geologically, it encompasses the Clarion fracture zone and the Clipperton fracture zone, which are prominent geological submarine fracture zones. The CCZ extends approximately 4,500 miles (7,240 km) east to west, covering an area of about 4,500,000 square kilometers (1,700,000 mi²), with depths generally ranging from 4,000 to 6,000 meters (9,800 to 19,700 ft). Scientific surveys have revealed that the CCZ harbors an abundance and diversity of deep-sea life.

**Clarion fracture zone**
The Clarion fracture zone is one of the significant geological submarine fracture zones located within the broader Clarion–Clipperton fracture zone (CCZ) in the northern Pacific Ocean. It is one of the five major lineations of the northern Pacific floor, discovered by the Scripps Institution of Oceanography in 1954. These oceanic fracture zones are typically long, linear features on the seafloor, often associated with transform faults that offset segments of mid-ocean ridges. Within the CCZ, the Clarion fracture zone contributes to the region's unusually mountainous topographical features and its rich deposits of polymetallic nodules.

**Clipperton fracture zone**
The Clipperton fracture zone is another prominent geological submarine fracture zone that forms part of the Clarion–Clipperton fracture zone (CCZ) in the eastern and central Pacific Ocean. Like the Clarion fracture zone, it is one of the five major lineations of the northern Pacific floor, identified in 1954. These fracture zones are critical components of the oceanic crust, representing areas where the seafloor has been fractured and displaced. The Clipperton fracture zone, along with the Clarion fracture zone, contributes to the complex topography and the significant mineral resources found within the CCZ.

**Niger Red Cross Society**
The Niger Red Cross Society (Croix-Rouge Nigérienne) is the national Red Cross society of Niger, headquartered in Niamey, the capital city. As an independent, voluntary aid society, it is an integral part of the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement, adhering to its fundamental principles of humanity, impartiality, neutrality, independence, voluntary service, unity, and universality. The society plays a crucial role in providing humanitarian assistance across Niger, particularly in areas such as disaster preparedness and response, health services, first aid training, and community development programs. It works to alleviate human suffering and protect life and health, often in collaboration with the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (IFRC) and other international partners, while maintaining its national operational autonomy and headquarters within Niger.

**Horizon Zero Dawn**
Joris De Man is a prominent composer who contributed significantly to the soundtrack of *Horizon Zero Dawn*, an action role-playing game developed by Guerrilla Games and published by Sony Interactive Entertainment in 2017. This game marked the first installment in the Horizon video game series and is set in a post-apocalyptic United States where large robotic machines dominate the Earth, and humans live in primitive tribes. The game follows Aloy, a young huntress, as she embarks on a quest to uncover her past, utilizing ranged weapons, a spear, and stealth to combat mechanical creatures and other enemy forces. De Man's musical contributions helped define the game's atmospheric and adventurous tone, complementing its vibrant colors and post-apocalyptic setting designed to evoke a sense of hope.

**Horizon Forbidden West**
Joris De Man also composed music for *Horizon Forbidden West*, the highly anticipated 2022 sequel to *Horizon Zero Dawn*. Developed by Guerrilla Games and published by Sony Interactive Entertainment, this action role-playing game continues Aloy's journey into a post-apocalyptic version of the Western United States, which is recovering from an extinction event caused by a rogue robot swarm. In *Forbidden West*, Aloy must venture into the titular Forbidden West to find the source of a mysterious plague. The game expanded upon its predecessor with new gameplay mechanics, including new traversal tools, underwater exploration, and expanded melee combat. De Man's score for *Forbidden West* further enriched the game's immersive open world and intense combat encounters, building upon the established musical themes of the franchise while introducing new elements to match the expanded scope and narrative.

The provided Wikipedia passages do not contain information about the municipality of Estavayer or its former name. Therefore, the following information is supplemented from encyclopedic knowledge.

**Estavayer-le-Lac**
The municipality of Estavayer in Switzerland was formerly known as **Estavayer-le-Lac**. This name was used until January 1, 2017, when it merged with the former municipalities of Bussy, Morens, Murist, Rueyres-les-Prés, Vernay, and Vuissens to form the new municipality of Estavayer. The "le-Lac" suffix, meaning "on the lake," referred to its location on the southern shore of Lake Neuchâtel. Estavayer-le-Lac was historically a significant town in the canton of Fribourg, known for its well-preserved medieval old town, its castle (Château de Chenaux), and its strategic position. The name change reflects the broader trend in Swiss municipal reforms aimed at consolidating smaller administrative units into larger, more efficient entities.

The narrative centers on the life of a talented cricketer from a humble background who faces numerous challenges and systemic biases in his pursuit of a career in professional cricket. D. wikipedia_gemini-2.5-flash_self_containe:
Here are the films that were directed by Terence Young and written by Richard Maibaum:

**Dr. No**
*Dr. No* is a 1962 spy film and holds the distinction of being the first film in the iconic James Bond series. It was directed by Terence Young and adapted for the screen by Richard Maibaum, Johanna Harwood, and Berkely Mather, based on Ian Fleming's 1958 novel. The film stars Sean Connery as James Bond, who is sent to Jamaica to investigate the disappearance of a fellow British agent. His investigation leads him to the underground lair of the titular villain, Dr. No, who is plotting to disrupt an early American space launch using a radio beam weapon. Produced by Harry Saltzman and Albert R. Broccoli of Eon Productions, *Dr. No* was a financial success upon its release on October 5, 1962, despite being made on a low budget.

**From Russia with Love**
*From Russia with Love* is a 1963 spy film, serving as the second installment in the James Bond series. It was directed by Terence Young and written by Richard Maibaum and Johanna Harwood, adapting Ian Fleming's 1957 novel of the same name. Sean Connery reprises his role as MI6 agent James Bond. In this film, Bond is dispatched to Istanbul to assist in the defection of Soviet consulate clerk Tatiana Romanova, only to find himself entangled in a plot by SPECTRE, which seeks to avenge Bond's previous actions against Dr. No. Following the success of *Dr. No*, United Artists doubled the budget for this sequel, allowing for location filming in Turkey, in addition to scenes shot at Pinewood Studios. *From Russia with Love* proved to be a critical and commercial blockbuster, significantly outperforming its predecessor with over $78 million in worldwide box-office receipts against a $2 million budget.

**Thunderball**
*Thunderball* is a 1965 spy film and the fourth entry in the James Bond series, produced by Eon Productions. It was the third and final Bond film directed by Terence Young, with the screenplay penned by Richard Maibaum and John Hopkins. The film stars Sean Connery as James Bond and is an adaptation of Ian Fleming's 1961 novel, which itself was based on an original screenplay by Jack Whittingham, conceived from a story by Kevin McClory, Whittingham, and Fleming. The plot sees Bond on a mission to recover two NATO atomic bombs stolen by SPECTRE, which is holding the world ransom for £100 million in diamonds, threatening to destroy a major metropolis. Bond's investigation leads him to the Bahamas, where he confronts Emilio Largo, SPECTRE Number Two, culminating in an extensive underwater battle. The film's complex production involved four different units, with approximately a quarter of the film dedicated to underwater sequences.

wikipedia_gemini-2.5-flash_self_containe:
Here are the Family Computer games that featured the character Mario, based on the provided passages and supplemented with encyclopedic knowledge:

**Super Mario Bros. (1985)**
*Super Mario Bros.*, released in 1985, is the seminal platform game that launched the flagship *Super Mario* series and prominently featured Mario as its protagonist. While the provided passages mention its release year and its status as the start of the *Super Mario* series, it is widely known through encyclopedic knowledge that this game was originally developed and published by Nintendo for the Family Computer (Famicom) in Japan. It quickly became a smash hit, establishing the core gameplay mechanics of the series, which typically rely on Mario's jumping ability to navigate the fictional Mushroom Kingdom and progress through levels.

**Super Mario Bros. 2 (Japanese version, 1986)**
Following the immense success of the original, Nintendo swiftly released a direct sequel in Japan in 1986, titled *Super Mario Bros. 2*. This game, often referred to as *Super Mario Bros.: The Lost Levels* in Western markets, was an adaptation of the original *Super Mario Bros.* but featured significantly advanced difficulty. It was released for the Family Computer Disk System, an add-on for the Famicom console. This Japanese sequel continued Mario's adventures, presenting players with more challenging level designs and new gameplay elements, and was specifically designed for the Japanese market before Nintendo of America deemed it too difficult for Western audiences.

**Super Mario USA (1992)**
*Super Mario USA* is a 1992 re-release for the Famicom in Japan. This game is actually the Japanese version of what was released in the West as *Super Mario Bros. 2* (the one based on *Yume Kōjō: Doki Doki Panic*). After the Western *Super Mario Bros. 2* proved to be a success and differentiated the *Super Mario* series, Nintendo re-released it in Japan under the title *Super Mario USA*. In this version, Mario, along with Luigi, Princess Peach, and Toad, embarks on an adventure through the dream world of Subcon to defeat the villainous Wart, showcasing a distinct gameplay style compared to the original Japanese *Super Mario Bros. 2*.

wikipedia_gemini-2.5-flash_self_containe:
The provided Wikipedia passages do not contain information about Lillian Hellman or the films she screenwrote. Therefore, the following response is generated using encyclopedic knowledge to identify the directors of films for which Lillian Hellman served as the screenwriter.

**William Wyler**
William Wyler (1902–1981) was a German-American film director known for his meticulous filmmaking style and his ability to elicit strong performances from actors. He directed two films that were screenwritten by Lillian Hellman. The first was *The Little Foxes* (1941), an adaptation of Hellman's own acclaimed play, which earned nine Academy Award nominations, including Best Picture and Best Director. Wyler later collaborated with Hellman again on *The Children's Hour* (1961), a drama based on Hellman's controversial 1934 play *The Children's Hour*, for which Hellman also wrote the screenplay. Both collaborations showcased Wyler's skill in bringing Hellman's intense dramatic narratives to the screen.

**Lewis Milestone**
Lewis Milestone (1895–1980) was a Russian-American film director, celebrated for his versatility across various genres, including war films, dramas, and comedies. He directed *The North Star* (1943), a World War II propaganda film for which Lillian Hellman wrote the original screenplay. The film depicted the lives of Ukrainian villagers under Nazi occupation and their resistance efforts. Milestone's direction brought Hellman's narrative to life, contributing to the Allied war effort by portraying the resilience and heroism of the Soviet people.

**Herman Shumlin**
Herman Shumlin (1898–1979) was an American theatrical producer and director who also ventured into film direction. He directed the film adaptation of *Watch on the Rhine* (1943), a drama based on Lillian Hellman's Pulitzer Prize-winning play of the same name. Hellman herself wrote the screenplay for the film, which tells the story of an anti-fascist German living in exile in the United States during World War II. Shumlin, having directed the original Broadway production, was uniquely positioned to translate Hellman's powerful anti-fascist message and characterizations from stage to screen.

**William Dieterle**
William Dieterle (1893–1972) was a German-American film director known for his visually distinctive and often socially conscious films. He directed *The Searching Wind* (1946), a drama based on Lillian Hellman's play, for which Hellman also penned the screenplay. The film explored the moral compromises and political apathy of an American family in the years leading up to World War II, reflecting Hellman's strong anti-fascist sentiments. Dieterle's direction helped convey the complex emotional and political themes inherent in Hellman's script.

**Michael Gordon**
Michael Gordon (1909–1993) was an American film director who worked across various genres, including film noir, comedies, and dramas. He directed *Another Part of the Forest* (1948), a prequel to Lillian Hellman's *The Little Foxes*, for which Hellman also wrote the screenplay. The film delved into the earlier lives of the Hubbard family, exploring the origins of their greed and malice. Gordon's direction brought out the dark, psychological undercurrents of Hellman's narrative, further developing the complex family dynamics she had established in her earlier work.

*Odongo* is a 1956 British adventure film that was a production of Warwick Films and directed by John Gilling. The film stars Macdonald Carey, Rhonda Fleming, and Juma, and is set in East Africa. The narrative centers on a young boy named Odongo and his chimpanzee, whose lives become intertwined with a big-game hunter and a woman operating an animal orphanage. Warwick Films, founded by Irving Allen and Albert R. Broccoli, was known for its adventure and action films, often distributed by Columbia Pictures, which was also the distributor for *Odongo*. John Gilling, a prolific British director, helmed this project, contributing to his diverse filmography that spanned various genres, including adventure, crime, and later, horror films for companies like Hammer Film Productions.

wikipedia_gemini-2.5-flash_self_containe:
Here is a comprehensive and accurate response to your question, based primarily on the provided Wikipedia passages:

**James Bond film series**
The James Bond film series is a British series of spy films centered on the fictional MI6 agent James Bond, also known as "007." This long-running franchise is based on a series of books by Ian Fleming and is one of the longest continually running film series in history, having been in ongoing production from 1962 to the present, with a brief hiatus between 1989 and 1995. Eon Productions has been the primary company behind these films, producing 25 films as of 2021, most of which were made at Pinewood Studios. Harry Saltzman, in partnership with Albert R. Broccoli, was instrumental in co-producing the initial films of this highly successful series, which has grossed over $7 billion, making it the fifth-highest-grossing film series.

**Harry Saltzman**
Herschel "Harry" Saltzman was a Canadian theatre and film producer, best known for his pivotal role in co-producing the James Bond film series. He partnered with Albert R. Broccoli to produce the first nine films in the series for Eon Productions. Saltzman's involvement was crucial in establishing the cinematic legacy of James Bond, a character who originated in Ian Fleming's novels. His co-production tenure lasted until 1975, after which Albert R. Broccoli became the sole producer for most Eon films. Saltzman lived most of his life in Denham, Buckinghamshire, England, apart from a ten-year period in St. Petersburg, Florida.

**Eon Productions**
Eon Productions is the British film production company primarily responsible for the James Bond film series. It has been in ongoing production of these films since 1962, making it one of the longest-running film series in history. Eon Productions has produced 25 James Bond films as of 2021, with most of them being filmed at Pinewood Studios. The company was founded by Albert R. Broccoli and Harry Saltzman, who co-produced most of the Eon films until 1975. After Saltzman's departure, Broccoli continued as the sole producer, and later his stepson Michael G. Wilson and daughter Barbara Broccoli took over the production reins, ensuring the continuity of the franchise under the Danjaq family company.

**Albert R. Broccoli**
Albert R. Broccoli was an American film producer who, alongside Harry Saltzman, co-produced most of the early James Bond films for Eon Productions. Their partnership was foundational to the success and longevity of the James Bond film series, which began in 1962. Broccoli and Saltzman co-produced the Eon films until 1975, with the single exception of *Thunderball*, where they served as executive producers. Following Saltzman's departure, Broccoli became the sole producer, and later brought his stepson Michael G. Wilson into the production team in 1984. His daughter, Barbara Broccoli, took over from him in 1995, continuing the family's involvement in co-producing the series with Wilson, through their family company, Danjaq.

**Ian Fleming**
Ian Fleming was a British writer, journalist, and naval intelligence officer, best known as the creator of the fictional British Secret Service agent James Bond. The James Bond film series, produced by Eon Productions, Harry Saltzman, and Albert R. Broccoli, is based on the character and stories that originally appeared in Fleming's series of books. His literary creations provided the foundational material for one of the most enduring and successful film franchises in cinematic history, establishing the character of "007" as a global cultural icon.

**James Bond (character)**
James Bond is the fictional protagonist of the long-running British spy film series produced by Eon Productions. Known by his code number "007," he is an MI6 agent who originally appeared in a series of books written by Ian Fleming. The character is central to the film series co-produced by Harry Saltzman and Albert R. Broccoli, embodying the archetype of the sophisticated, daring, and resourceful secret agent. Six different actors have portrayed 007 in the Eon series, with Daniel Craig being the latest.

**The Man with the Golden Gun (film)**
*The Man with the Golden Gun* is a 1974 spy film, notable as the ninth installment in the James Bond series produced by Eon Productions. It was the second film to star Roger Moore as James Bond and was co-produced during Harry Saltzman's tenure with Albert R. Broccoli. Directed by Guy Hamilton, it was a loose adaptation of Ian Fleming's posthumously published 1965 novel. The film features Bond pursuing the assassin Francisco Scaramanga while attempting to secure the Solex Agitator, a technological solution to energy shortages, a theme reflecting the 1973 energy crisis. The film also incorporated elements of the then-popular martial arts craze and was set and shot predominantly in Asian locations like Thailand, Hong Kong, and Macau.

However, drawing upon general encyclopedic knowledge, the Eurovision Young Dancers is a biennial dance competition organised by the European Broadcasting Union (EBU) for young classical and contemporary dancers aged between 16 and 21. Numerous countries, represented by their national broadcasters, have participated in this event since its inception in 1985. Below are some examples of entities (countries) that have participated:

**Spain**
Spain is one of the most successful participating entities in the history of the Eurovision Young Dancers competition. Represented by its national broadcaster Televisión Española (TVE), Spain has won the contest a record five times, demonstrating a strong tradition in classical and contemporary dance. Their victories include the inaugural contest in 1985, and subsequent wins in 1991, 1993, 1995, and 1997, making them a dominant force in the early years of the competition.

**Sweden**
Sweden, represented by Sveriges Television (SVT), has been a consistent participant in the Eurovision Young Dancers competition since its debut. The country has hosted the event twice, in 1985 (the inaugural contest, held in Reggio Emilia, Italy, but co-produced by SVT) and 2003 (held in Lyon, France, but also co-produced by SVT). While not as dominant in terms of wins as Spain, Sweden has frequently reached the finals and showcased a high standard of young dance talent.

**Poland**
Poland, represented by Telewizja Polska (TVP), has also achieved significant success in the Eurovision Young Dancers contest. The country has won the competition three times, in 2001, 2005, and 2017, making it one of the most successful nations. Poland has also had the honour of hosting the event twice, in 1997 and 2005, further cementing its commitment to promoting young dance talent within the European broadcasting sphere.

**Norway**
Norway, represented by Norsk Rikskringkasting (NRK), has been a regular participant in the Eurovision Young Dancers competition. The country hosted the event in 1999 in Lyon, France, showcasing its involvement in the organisation of the contest. While Norway has not secured a victory in the competition, its participation has provided a platform for many talented young Norwegian dancers to gain international exposure and experience in a prestigious European setting.

**Germany**
Germany, represented by Westdeutscher Rundfunk (WDR) and other regional broadcasters within ARD, has been a long-standing participant in the Eurovision Young Dancers competition. The country has hosted the event on multiple occasions, including in 1987, 1989, 2009, and 2011, demonstrating its strong support for the initiative. German dancers have consistently competed, contributing to the diversity and high artistic standard of the competition over the decades.

**Ukraine**
Ukraine, represented by the Public Broadcasting Company of Ukraine (UA:PBC), has also participated in the Eurovision Young Dancers competition. While the provided passages mention UA:PBC as the host broadcaster for the Eurovision Song Contest 2017 and its withdrawal from the 2019 contest, it has also extended its participation to other EBU events like Young Dancers. Ukraine's involvement provides its young dancers with opportunities to compete on an international stage, fostering cultural exchange and artistic development.

**Peter Farrelly**
Peter Farrelly is an American film director, screenwriter, producer, and novelist, widely known for his work in comedy alongside his brother, Bobby Farrelly. John J. Strauss co-wrote the screenplay for the highly successful 1998 romantic comedy *There's Something About Mary*, which Peter Farrelly co-directed with his brother. The film, starring Ben Stiller, Cameron Diaz, and Matt Dillon, became a cultural phenomenon and a significant box office hit, solidifying the Farrelly brothers' reputation for distinctive, often raunchy, humor. Strauss's contribution as a screenwriter was integral to crafting the film's memorable comedic situations and character dynamics.

**Bobby Farrelly**
Bobby Farrelly is an American film director, screenwriter, and producer, who frequently collaborates with his brother, Peter Farrelly. Together, they directed numerous popular comedies, including *There's Something About Mary*, for which John J. Strauss served as a co-screenwriter. Released in 1998, this romantic comedy was a critical and commercial success, praised for its unique blend of gross-out humor and genuine heart. Bobby Farrelly's directorial vision, combined with the screenplay co-written by Strauss, helped define the film's iconic comedic style and enduring appeal.

**Michael Lembeck**
Michael Lembeck is an American actor and director, known for his work in both television and film. He directed *The Santa Clause 2* (2002), a family-friendly Christmas comedy for which John J. Strauss was one of the credited screenwriters. This sequel to the popular *The Santa Clause* saw Tim Allen reprise his role as Scott Calvin/Santa Claus, navigating new challenges related to his contractual obligations. Lembeck also directed *The Santa Clause 3: The Escape Clause* (2006), another film co-written by Strauss, further establishing their collaboration on the successful holiday franchise.

**Jim Fall**
Jim Fall is an American film director, known for his work on various comedies and family films. He directed *The Lizzie McGuire Movie* (2003), a musical teen comedy film that served as the cinematic conclusion to the popular Disney Channel series *Lizzie McGuire*. John J. Strauss was one of the screenwriters for this film, which followed Lizzie McGuire and her friends on a graduation trip to Rome, where Lizzie is mistaken for an Italian pop star. Fall's direction, combined with the screenplay co-written by Strauss, successfully translated the beloved television series to the big screen, appealing to its established fanbase.

**Sean McNamara**
Sean McNamara is an American film director, producer, and screenwriter, recognized for his work primarily in family-oriented and inspirational films. He directed *Soul Surfer* (2011), a biographical drama film based on the true story of Bethany Hamilton, a teenage surfer who lost her arm in a shark attack and overcame the odds to return to professional surfing. John J. Strauss was among the screenwriters who adapted Hamilton's autobiography for the screen. McNamara's direction brought this inspiring story to life, with Strauss's screenplay contributing to the narrative's emotional depth and authenticity.

wikipedia_gemini-2.5-flash_self_containe:
**Lake Zurich**
Lake Zurich (German: Zürichsee) is a large lake in Switzerland, situated southeast of the city of Zurich. It is a significant geographic entity directly connected to the Linth River, which is its primary inflow. The Linth River originates in the Glarus Alps and flows through the Linth Valley before entering Lake Zurich near the town of Schmerikon. This hydrological connection means that Lake Zurich is located next to and is an integral part of the Linth's course, serving as a natural reservoir for its waters. The lake then drains into the Limmat River, which flows through the city of Zurich. Lake Zurich is a prominent feature of the Swiss landscape, serving as a hub for recreation, transportation, and a vital source of drinking water for the surrounding metropolitan area.

wikipedia_gemini-2.5-flash_self_containe:
The provided Wikipedia passages do not contain information about the 1924 British Mount Everest Expedition. Therefore, the following information is supplemented from encyclopedic knowledge.

**George Mallory**
George Mallory was a prominent English mountaineer who participated in the 1924 British Mount Everest Expedition. He is most famously known for his attempts to be among the first to summit Mount Everest and for his disappearance along with his climbing partner, Andrew "Sandy" Irvine, during their final ascent attempt on June 8, 1924. Mallory's body was discovered in 1999 at an altitude of 8,155 meters (26,755 ft) on the north face of the mountain, sparking renewed debate about whether he and Irvine might have reached the summit before perishing, 29 years before Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay's confirmed ascent in 1953.

**Andrew "Sandy" Irvine**
Andrew "Sandy" Irvine was a British mountaineer who was George Mallory's climbing partner during the 1924 British Mount Everest Expedition. Irvine, a younger and less experienced climber than Mallory, was particularly skilled with oxygen equipment, which was a relatively new technology at the time. He disappeared with Mallory during their summit attempt on June 8, 1924, and his body has never been found. The question of whether Mallory and Irvine reached the summit before their deaths remains one of mountaineering's most enduring mysteries, with Irvine's camera, which might hold crucial evidence, still missing.

**Edward Felix Norton**
Edward Felix Norton was a British mountaineer and military officer who served as a member of the 1924 British Mount Everest Expedition. During the expedition, Norton achieved a significant mountaineering feat by reaching a then-record altitude of 8,570 meters (28,126 ft) without supplemental oxygen on June 4, 1924. This was the highest point reached by a human being for nearly 30 years and demonstrated the extreme limits of human endurance at high altitudes. His solo ascent without oxygen was a remarkable achievement, though he was forced to turn back due to exhaustion and snow blindness.

**Howard Somervell**
Howard Somervell was a British surgeon and mountaineer who participated in the 1924 British Mount Everest Expedition. Alongside Edward Felix Norton, Somervell made a strong attempt on the summit, reaching an altitude of 8,570 meters (28,126 ft) on June 4, 1924, before turning back. He was a key member of the climbing team and played a crucial role in the expedition's efforts to reach the summit. Somervell's contributions included his medical expertise, which was vital for the health and safety of the team in the harsh Himalayan environment, and his strong climbing performance.

**Noel Odell**
Noel Odell was a British geologist and mountaineer who was a member of the 1924 British Mount Everest Expedition. He is particularly known for being the last person to see George Mallory and Andrew Irvine alive, observing them through a break in the clouds "going strong for the top" at approximately 12:50 PM on June 8, 1924, high on the northeast ridge. Odell himself made a significant solo ascent to search for Mallory and Irvine after their disappearance, reaching a high altitude and enduring extreme conditions. His geological observations also contributed to the scientific understanding of the Everest region.

wikipedia_gemini-2.5-flash_self_containe:
**Hatsuharu**
Hatsuharu was the lead ship of the Hatsuharu-class destroyers, built for the Imperial Japanese Navy (IJN). As the second IJN destroyer to bear this name, it was part of a class of six vessels constructed under the Circle One Program (Maru Ichi Keikaku). Hatsuharu itself was laid down in JFY 1931. These destroyers were designed to serve the IJN before and throughout World War II, with Hatsuharu providing the foundational design for the subsequent ships in its class.

**Nenohi**
Nenohi was one of the six destroyers belonging to the Hatsuharu class of the Imperial Japanese Navy. Like its lead ship, Hatsuharu, Nenohi was among the first three vessels of the class to be laid down in JFY 1931 under the Circle One Program. These destroyers were integral to the IJN's fleet, operating both before and during World War II.

**Wakaba**
Wakaba was another destroyer within the Hatsuharu class of the Imperial Japanese Navy. It was part of the initial group of three ships in the class, laid down in JFY 1931 as part of the Circle One Program. The Hatsuharu-class destroyers, including Wakaba, were active in the IJN's operations leading up to and throughout World War II.

**Hatsushimo**
Hatsushimo was a destroyer of the Hatsuharu class, serving in the Imperial Japanese Navy. It was one of the six ships in the class, specifically part of the second batch of three vessels that were laid down in JFY 1933, following the initial construction phase. These destroyers were deployed by the IJN before and during World War II.

**Ariake**
Ariake was a destroyer of the Hatsuharu class, built for the Imperial Japanese Navy and serving before and during World War II. It was one of the six ships in the class, and notably, one of the final two vessels. These last two ships, including Ariake, were completed after significant modifications to the original design, leading them to sometimes be considered a separate "Ariake class" within the broader Hatsuharu designation. Ariake was laid down in JFY 1933.

**Yūgure**
Yūgure was a destroyer belonging to the Hatsuharu class of the Imperial Japanese Navy, active before and during World War II. As one of the six ships in the class, Yūgure, along with Ariake, represented the final two vessels constructed. These two ships incorporated design modifications that led to them occasionally being referred to as a distinct "Ariake class." Yūgure was laid down in JFY 1933.

**German Type IID Submarine Class**
The German Type IID submarine was a class of small coastal submarines (U-boats) built for the Kriegsmarine (German Navy) during World War II. These submarines were an improved version of the Type IIC, featuring an additional "saddle tank" that increased their fuel capacity and, consequently, their operational range. Designed primarily for coastal operations and training, the Type IID U-boats were part of the larger Type II series, often referred to as "canoes" due to their small size. A total of 14 Type IID submarines were built, serving mainly in the Baltic Sea for training purposes and, at times, in limited combat roles in the North Sea and English Channel.

**U-137**
U-137 was a German Type IID submarine of the Kriegsmarine during World War II. Commissioned on 16 November 1940, she was one of the fourteen boats of her class. Initially serving with the 1st U-boat Flotilla for training, U-137 later conducted several short combat patrols, primarily in the North Sea. She was scuttled by her crew on 2 May 1945 in Wilhelmshaven as part of Operation Regenbogen, to prevent her capture by Allied forces.

**U-138**
U-138 was a German Type IID submarine that served in the Kriegsmarine during World War II. Launched on 18 November 1940, she was part of the Type IID class, known for its extended range compared to earlier Type II variants. U-138 participated in several combat patrols, sinking a few Allied merchant ships. She was scuttled on 5 May 1945 in Wesermünde, Germany, following the German surrender, to avoid falling into Allied hands.

**U-139**
U-139 was a German Type IID submarine commissioned into the Kriegsmarine on 14 December 1940. As a member of the Type IID class, she was designed for coastal operations and training. U-139 primarily served as a training boat in the Baltic Sea with the 22nd U-boat Flotilla. She did not undertake any combat patrols and was scuttled by her crew on 2 May 1945 in Wilhelmshaven, Germany, at the end of the war.

**U-140**
U-140 was a German Type IID submarine of the Kriegsmarine, commissioned on 18 December 1940. This U-boat belonged to the Type IID class, which featured improved fuel capacity for extended range. U-140 spent most of her career as a training vessel in the Baltic Sea. She was scuttled on 2 May 1945 in Wilhelmshaven, Germany, as part of the scuttling of the German fleet to prevent capture by the Allies.

**U-141**
U-141 was a German Type IID submarine that served in the Kriegsmarine during World War II. Commissioned on 16 January 1941, she was one of the fourteen Type IID boats. U-141 conducted a few combat patrols in the North Atlantic and North Sea, sinking a small number of Allied ships. After her combat career, she transitioned to a training role. U-141 was scuttled on 2 May 1945 in Wilhelmshaven, Germany.

**U-142**
U-142 was a German Type IID submarine of the Kriegsmarine, commissioned on 1 February 1941. As a Type IID, she was a small coastal submarine primarily intended for training and limited combat operations. U-142 spent her entire service life as a training boat in the Baltic Sea, attached to the 22nd U-boat Flotilla. She was scuttled on 2 May 1945 in Wilhelmshaven, Germany, to prevent her capture by Allied forces.

**U-143**
U-143 was a German Type IID submarine that entered service with the Kriegsmarine on 8 February 1941. Belonging to the Type IID class, she was designed for coastal defense and training. U-143 primarily served as a training vessel in the Baltic Sea. She was scuttled on 2 May 1945 in Wilhelmshaven, Germany, as part of the widespread scuttling of the German U-boat fleet at the end of World War II.

**U-144**
U-144 was a German Type IID submarine commissioned into the Kriegsmarine on 19 February 1941. This U-boat was part of the Type IID class, characterized by its increased range compared to earlier Type II models. U-144 conducted several combat patrols in the Baltic Sea and was sunk on 10 August 1941 by the Soviet submarine Shch-307, becoming one of the few Type IID boats lost in combat.

**U-145**
U-145 was a German Type IID submarine that served in the Kriegsmarine during World War II. Commissioned on 8 March 1941, she was one of the fourteen Type IID submarines. U-145 primarily operated as a training boat in the Baltic Sea. She was scuttled on 2 May 1945 in Wilhelmshaven, Germany, by her crew to prevent her capture by the advancing Allied forces.

**U-146**
U-146 was a German Type IID submarine of the Kriegsmarine, commissioned on 22 March 1941. As a Type IID, she was a small coastal submarine designed for training and limited combat. U-146 spent most of her career as a training vessel in the Baltic Sea. She was scuttled on 2 May 1945 in Wilhelmshaven, Germany, as part of Operation Regenbogen, the scuttling of the German fleet.

**U-147**
U-147 was a German Type IID submarine that entered service with the Kriegsmarine on 11 April 1941. Belonging to the Type IID class, she was intended for coastal operations and training. U-147 conducted a few combat patrols in the North Atlantic, but was sunk on 2 June 1941 by the British destroyer HMS Wanderer and the corvette HMS Periwinkle, becoming one of the earliest Type IID losses.

**U-148**
U-148 was a German Type IID submarine commissioned into the Kriegsmarine on 26 April 1941. This U-boat was part of the Type IID class, known for its improved range. U-148 primarily served as a training boat in the Baltic Sea with the 22nd U-boat Flotilla. She was scuttled on 2 May 1945 in Wilhelmshaven, Germany, to prevent her capture by Allied forces at the end of World War II.

**U-149**
U-149 was a German Type IID submarine that served in the Kriegsmarine during World War II. Commissioned on 10 May 1941, she was one of the fourteen Type IID submarines. U-149 spent her entire service life as a training boat in the Baltic Sea. She was scuttled on 2 May 1945 in Wilhelmshaven, Germany, by her crew as part of the scuttling orders issued by Admiral Karl Dönitz.

**U-150**
U-150 was a German Type IID submarine of the Kriegsmarine, commissioned on 28 May 1941. As the last of the Type IID class, she was a small coastal submarine primarily used for training. U-150 served exclusively as a training boat in the Baltic Sea. She was scuttled on 2 May 1945 in Wilhelmshaven, Germany, along with many other German U-boats, to prevent their capture by the Allies.

Bhaskaran's extensive work as a filmmaker and lyricist, including directing 44 Malayalam feature films, but do not list any of his film titles or their music composers, nor do they mention V. Dakshinamoorthy.

Based on encyclopedic knowledge, P. Bhaskaran and V. Dakshinamoorthy collaborated on numerous Malayalam films. Below are some notable examples:

**Adimakal (1969)**
*Adimakal* is a critically acclaimed 1969 Indian Malayalam-language drama film directed by P. Bhaskaran. The film's music was composed by V. Dakshinamoorthy, with lyrics penned by P. Bhaskaran himself. Starring Sathyan, Prem Nazir, Sharada, and Jayabharathi, the movie explores themes of social hierarchy and human relationships, set against the backdrop of a rural community. It was a significant commercial and critical success, earning the National Film Award for Best Feature Film in Malayalam and the Kerala State Film Award for Best Film. The collaboration between P. Bhaskaran's direction and V. Dakshinamoorthy's melodious compositions, particularly the songs, contributed significantly to its enduring popularity.

**Iruttinte Athmavu (1967)**
*Iruttinte Athmavu* (meaning "The Soul of Darkness") is a 1967 Indian Malayalam-language drama film directed by P. Bhaskaran. V. Dakshinamoorthy composed the music for this film, which is renowned for its powerful storytelling and performances. The film features Prem Nazir in a critically acclaimed role as a mentally challenged young man, showcasing a departure from his usual romantic hero image. P. Bhaskaran's sensitive direction, combined with Dakshinamoorthy's evocative musical score, helped to create a poignant and memorable cinematic experience that explored themes of mental illness and societal attitudes towards it.

**Thulabharam (1968)**
*Thulabharam* is a 1968 Indian Malayalam-language drama film directed by P. Bhaskaran. The musical score for the film was provided by V. Dakshinamoorthy, with lyrics by P. Bhaskaran. The film starred Sharada, Prem Nazir, and Madhu, and is particularly noted for Sharada's performance, which earned her the National Film Award for Best Actress. *Thulabharam* delves into the struggles of a working-class family facing poverty and injustice, culminating in a tragic climax. The film's success was a testament to the strong collaboration between Bhaskaran's direction and Dakshinamoorthy's music, which effectively conveyed the emotional depth of the narrative.

wikipedia_gemini-2.5-flash_self_containe:
Here is an exhaustive list of the Governors of the State Bank of Pakistan, including the individual mentioned in the provided passages and supplementing with encyclopedic knowledge for a comprehensive overview:

**Zahid Husain**
Zahid Husain served as the inaugural Governor of the State Bank of Pakistan, holding the position from July 1948 to July 1953. As the first head of the newly established central bank, his tenure was crucial in laying the foundational framework for Pakistan's monetary and financial system following the country's independence. He was instrumental in setting up the bank's operations, establishing its policies, and guiding the nascent economy through its initial challenges. His leadership helped define the role and functions of the State Bank as the primary monetary authority.

**Abdul Qadir**
Abdul Qadir succeeded Zahid Husain, serving as Governor from July 1953 to July 1957. During his term, the State Bank continued to develop its regulatory and supervisory functions, adapting to the evolving economic landscape of Pakistan. His contributions focused on strengthening the banking sector and ensuring financial stability, which was vital for the country's economic growth and development in its early years.

**S. A. Hasnie**
S. A. Hasnie held the governorship from August 1957 to February 1960. His tenure was marked by efforts to consolidate the financial infrastructure and implement policies aimed at fostering economic stability. As Pakistan's economy began to diversify, Hasnie's leadership at the State Bank played a role in managing monetary policy to support industrialization and agricultural development.

**Mahbubur Rahman**
Mahbubur Rahman served as Governor from March 1960 to January 1967, making him one of the longest-serving governors in the bank's early history. His period saw significant economic planning and development initiatives in Pakistan. Under his guidance, the State Bank continued to evolve its role in economic management, including foreign exchange management and credit allocation to priority sectors, contributing to the country's economic growth during the 1960s.

**M. Raschid**
M. Raschid was the Governor of the State Bank of Pakistan from February 1967 to August 1971. His tenure coincided with a period of political and economic turbulence leading up to the 1971 war and the separation of East Pakistan. During these challenging times, the State Bank, under Raschid's leadership, faced the complex task of maintaining financial stability and managing the monetary system amidst significant national upheaval.

**Shakirullah Durrani**
Shakirullah Khan Durrani, also known as Shakir K. Durrani, served as the Governor of the State Bank of Pakistan from September 1971 to December 1971. A prominent Pakistani banker, he also held the position of managing director of Pakistan International Airlines. His brief tenure as Governor occurred during a critical juncture in Pakistan's history, immediately preceding and during the 1971 war. Durrani's daughter, Tehmina Durrani, is married to former Chief Minister of Punjab and current Prime Minister of Pakistan, Shehbaz Sharif.

**Ghulam Ishaq Khan**
Ghulam Ishaq Khan, a highly influential figure in Pakistani politics and bureaucracy, served as Governor from December 1971 to July 1978. His tenure was significant as it encompassed the period of nationalization under President Zulfikar Ali Bhutto, which considerably enlarged the scope of the State Bank's functions. Khan played a crucial role in implementing the economic policies of the time, including the nationalization of financial services, manufacturing, and transportation industries. He later went on to become the President of Pakistan.

**S. Osman Ali**
S. Osman Ali served as Governor from July 1978 to July 1981. His term coincided with Pakistan's transition towards an Islamic economic system in the following decade, which involved outlawing economic practices forbidden under Sharia law. The State Bank, under his leadership, began to navigate the complexities of integrating Islamic financial principles into the country's banking and monetary framework.

**A. G. N. Kazi**
A. G. N. Kazi held the governorship from July 1981 to August 1986. His tenure was marked by continued efforts to implement Islamic banking principles and to manage the economy during a period of significant structural adjustments. Kazi's experience as a seasoned bureaucrat was instrumental in guiding the State Bank through these reforms and maintaining financial stability.

**I. A. Hanfi**
I. A. Hanfi served as Governor from August 1986 to August 1989. During his term, Pakistan's economy began to experience the initial phases of privatization, reversing some of the nationalization policies of the 1970s. The State Bank, under Hanfi, played a role in facilitating these economic shifts and managing the monetary implications of a more market-oriented approach.

**Kassim Parekh**
Kassim Parekh was the Governor from August 1989 to August 1993. His tenure was characterized by further liberalization and privatization efforts in the Pakistani economy. The State Bank continued to adapt its policies to support these reforms, aiming to foster a more dynamic and competitive financial sector.

**M. Yaqub**
M. Yaqub served as Governor from September 1993 to September 1999. His long tenure was a period of significant economic reforms and challenges, including efforts to stabilize the macroeconomy, control inflation, and strengthen the banking system. Yaqub's leadership was crucial in implementing structural adjustment programs and modernizing the central bank's operations.

**Muhammad Mian Soomro**
Muhammad Mian Soomro held the position of Governor from December 1999 to December 2000. His brief term occurred during a period of political transition and economic restructuring. Soomro, who later served as Chairman of the Senate and caretaker Prime Minister, brought his administrative experience to the central bank during a critical time.

**Ishrat Husain**
Ishrat Husain served as Governor from December 2000 to December 2005. His tenure is widely regarded as a period of significant reforms and modernization for the State Bank of Pakistan. He implemented policies aimed at strengthening financial sector stability, improving monetary policy transmission, and enhancing the bank's autonomy and governance. His efforts contributed to a period of sustained economic growth and improved financial health for the country.

**Shamshad Akhtar**
Shamshad Akhtar made history as the first woman to serve as Governor of the State Bank of Pakistan, holding the position from January 2006 to January 2009. Her tenure focused on further strengthening the financial sector, promoting financial inclusion, and enhancing the bank's regulatory and supervisory framework. She played a key role in navigating the global financial crisis of 2008 and implementing measures to safeguard Pakistan's economy.

**Salim Raza**
Salim Raza served as Governor from February 2009 to February 2010. His term was marked by the ongoing challenges of the global financial crisis and its impact on Pakistan's economy. The State Bank, under his leadership, continued to implement measures to ensure financial stability and support economic recovery.

**Yaseen Anwar**
Yaseen Anwar was the Governor from October 2011 to January 2014. His tenure focused on maintaining monetary stability, managing inflation, and supporting economic growth. He also emphasized strengthening the regulatory framework for banks and promoting financial inclusion initiatives.

**Ashraf Mahmood Wathra**
Ashraf Mahmood Wathra served as Governor from April 2014 to April 2017. During his term, the State Bank continued its efforts to stabilize the economy, manage foreign exchange reserves, and implement reforms to improve the banking sector's resilience. He also focused on enhancing the central bank's autonomy and transparency.

**Tariq Bajwa**
Tariq Bajwa held the governorship from July 2017 to July 2019. His tenure involved navigating economic challenges such as current account deficits and inflationary pressures. The State Bank, under his leadership, implemented monetary policy measures aimed at achieving macroeconomic stability and supporting sustainable economic growth.

**Reza Baqir**
Reza Baqir served as Governor from May 2019 to May 2022. His term was characterized by significant economic reforms, including efforts to stabilize the exchange rate, control inflation, and implement structural adjustments under an IMF program. He also focused on modernizing the payment system and promoting digital financial services.

**Jameel Ahmad**
Jameel Ahmad is the current Governor of the State Bank of Pakistan, having assumed office in August 2022. His tenure is focused on addressing ongoing economic challenges, including high inflation, exchange rate volatility, and external financing needs. The State Bank, under his leadership, is implementing tight monetary policies to curb inflation and stabilize the economy, while also working to strengthen the financial sector and promote sustainable growth.

**The Fugitive**
Quinn Martin was the executive producer of *The Fugitive*, an American drama series that aired on ABC from 1963 to 1967. The series starred David Janssen as Dr. Richard Kimble, a physician wrongly convicted of his wife's murder, who escapes from custody and travels the country in search of the real killer, a one-armed man, while being relentlessly pursued by Lieutenant Philip Gerard (Barry Morse). *The Fugitive* became one of the most iconic and critically acclaimed shows of its era, known for its suspenseful storytelling and serialized format, which was somewhat unusual for television at the time. The series concluded with a two-part finale that revealed the true killer, drawing one of the largest audiences in television history up to that point.

**The F.B.I.**
Quinn Martin also served as the executive producer for *The F.B.I.*, a crime drama television series that aired on ABC from 1965 to 1974. The show starred Efrem Zimbalist Jr. as Inspector Lewis Erskine, a dedicated agent of the Federal Bureau of Investigation, and was notable for its close cooperation with the actual FBI, including the approval of scripts by J. Edgar Hoover himself. Each episode typically depicted the FBI's efforts to solve a specific case, often involving organized crime, espionage, or other serious federal offenses. The series was a long-running success, reflecting the public's interest in law enforcement and the perceived integrity of the FBI during that period.

**The Streets of San Francisco**
Another prominent series executive produced by Quinn Martin was *The Streets of San Francisco*, a police procedural drama that aired on ABC from 1972 to 1977. The show starred Karl Malden as veteran Detective Lieutenant Mike Stone and Michael Douglas as his younger, college-educated partner, Inspector Steve Keller. Set against the backdrop of San Francisco, the series followed the duo as they investigated various crimes, from murders to kidnappings, often highlighting the city's diverse social landscape. *The Streets of San Francisco* was a popular and critically well-received series, known for its strong performances and realistic portrayal of police work, and it further solidified Quinn Martin's reputation for producing high-quality crime dramas.

**Cannon**
Quinn Martin was the executive producer of *Cannon*, an American detective series that aired on CBS from 1971 to 1976. The show featured William Conrad as Frank Cannon, a portly, bald, and expensive private investigator who was a former police officer. Unlike many of his contemporaries, Cannon was not a hard-boiled, street-level detective but rather a sophisticated, well-paid professional who often traveled to solve cases for wealthy clients. The series was known for its distinctive lead character and its focus on intricate plots, contributing to Quinn Martin's extensive portfolio of successful crime and mystery productions.

**Barnaby Jones**
*Barnaby Jones* was another detective series executive produced by Quinn Martin, which aired on CBS from 1973 to 1980. The show starred Buddy Ebsen as the titular character, a retired private detective who returns to the profession after his son is murdered. He works alongside his daughter-in-law, Betty Jones (Lee Meriwether), and later his cousin, J.R. Jones (Mark Shera). *Barnaby Jones* was a spin-off from *Cannon*, with Buddy Ebsen's character first appearing in an episode of that series. It became a long-running and popular show, known for its gentle yet astute protagonist and its focus on solving mysteries, further demonstrating Quinn Martin's consistent success in the crime drama genre.

**George A. Berlinghof**
George A. Berlinghof (1860–1944) was a prominent American architect, primarily active in Nebraska and the Midwest during the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Born in Germany, he immigrated to the United States and established a successful architectural practice, often working in partnership with other architects like Alfred H. Thomsen. Berlinghof was known for designing a wide range of buildings, including courthouses, schools, churches, commercial blocks, and residences, often incorporating elements of Romanesque Revival, Neoclassical, and Prairie School styles. His work significantly shaped the architectural landscape of many Nebraska towns and cities.

**Nebraska State Capitol (Original Design Competition)**
While the current Nebraska State Capitol building was designed by Bertram Goodhue, George A. Berlinghof was a significant participant in the design competition for the new capitol building in the early 20th century. Berlinghof, in partnership with Alfred H. Thomsen, submitted a design that was among the finalists. Although their design was not ultimately selected, their participation in such a high-profile competition demonstrates their standing and influence within the architectural community of Nebraska at the time.

**Lancaster County Courthouse (Lincoln, Nebraska)**
George A. Berlinghof, in partnership with Alfred H. Thomsen, designed the Lancaster County Courthouse in Lincoln, Nebraska. Completed in 1904, this impressive building is a notable example of Neoclassical architecture, featuring a grand dome and classical detailing. It served as the seat of county government for many decades and is recognized for its architectural significance and its role in the civic life of Lincoln.

**Gage County Courthouse (Beatrice, Nebraska)**
The Gage County Courthouse in Beatrice, Nebraska, is another significant work attributed to George A. Berlinghof. Designed in the Romanesque Revival style, this courthouse, completed in 1892, features robust masonry, prominent arches, and a distinctive clock tower. It stands as a testament to Berlinghof's early career and his ability to execute monumental public buildings that conveyed authority and permanence.

**York County Courthouse (York, Nebraska)**
George A. Berlinghof also designed the York County Courthouse in York, Nebraska. Completed in 1900, this building showcases a blend of Romanesque Revival and Neoclassical elements, characterized by its sturdy construction and classical ornamentation. Like his other courthouse designs, it served as a central hub for county administration and justice, reflecting the architectural trends of its era and Berlinghof's skill in public architecture.

**First National Bank Building (Lincoln, Nebraska)**
Berlinghof designed several commercial buildings, including the First National Bank Building in Lincoln, Nebraska. While specific details about all his commercial projects can be extensive, his work on such structures contributed to the urban fabric of Nebraska's growing cities. These buildings often featured ornate facades and robust construction, reflecting the prosperity and architectural ambitions of the businesses they housed.

**Various School Buildings**
Throughout his career, George A. Berlinghof designed numerous school buildings across Nebraska. These educational institutions, often built in a functional yet aesthetically pleasing manner, were crucial for the development of communities. His school designs typically incorporated durable materials and practical layouts, reflecting the evolving standards for public education facilities in the early 20th century.

**Churches and Residences**
Beyond public and commercial structures, Berlinghof also designed a number of churches and private residences. His ecclesiastical works often featured traditional architectural styles, while his residential designs could range from grand homes for prominent citizens to more modest dwellings, demonstrating his versatility as an architect. These projects contributed to the diverse architectural heritage of the communities he served.
